title: Inequalities & Absolute Value
blurb: Solve and graph inequalities, flip signs correctly, and master absolute value as distance.
theory:
What Is an Inequality?

An inequality compares two quantities that are not necessarily equal. The four symbols are: less than (<), greater than (>), less than or equal to (≤), and greater than or equal to (≥). A solution to an inequality is usually a range of values, not a single number.

For example, x > 3 means every number bigger than 3 works: 3.1, 4, 1000, and so on (but not 3 itself).

Solving Linear Inequalities

You solve a linear inequality almost exactly like an equation: add, subtract, multiply, or divide both sides to isolate the variable. There is one critical exception.

The Golden Rule: When you multiply or divide both sides by a negative number, you must flip the inequality sign.

StepResult
Start-2x < 6
Divide by -2 (flip!)x > -3

Why? Multiplying by a negative reverses order on the number line. Since 2 < 5 but -2 > -5, the direction reverses.

Compound Inequalities

A compound inequality such as a < x < b means x is between a and b. Whatever you do to the middle, do to all three parts.

Example: -1 < 2x + 3 ≤ 9. Subtract 3 from all parts: -4 < 2x ≤ 6. Divide all parts by 2: -2 < x ≤ 3.

Graphing on a Number Line

Use an open circle for < or > (endpoint not included) and a filled circle for ≤ or ≥ (endpoint included). Shade the region that satisfies the inequality.

  • x ≥ 2: filled dot at 2, shade right.
  • x < 5: open dot at 5, shade left.
  • -2 ≤ x < 4: filled dot at -2, open dot at 4, shade between.

Absolute Value as Distance

The absolute value |x| is the distance of x from 0 on the number line. Distance is never negative, so |x| ≥ 0 always. Both |5| and |-5| equal 5 because both are 5 units from zero.

More generally, |a - b| is the distance between a and b. This distance interpretation is the fastest way to solve absolute-value problems.

Solving Absolute Value Equations and Inequalities

Let k be a positive number. Memorize these three patterns:

FormMeaningSolution
|x| = kdistance from 0 equals kx = k or x = -k
|x| < kdistance less than k (inside)-k < x < k
|x| > kdistance more than k (outside)x < -k or x > k

A memory hook: "less-thand" gives an and (a single between-range), while "great-or" gives an or (two separate pieces).

For more complex expressions, isolate the absolute value first, then apply the pattern. Example: |2x - 3| ≤ 7 becomes -7 ≤ 2x - 3 ≤ 7, so -4 ≤ 2x ≤ 10, giving -2 ≤ x ≤ 5.

Special cases: |x| = -3 has no solution (distance cannot be negative). |x| > -3 is true for all x. |x| < -3 has no solution.

Ranges of Expressions

If you know the range of a variable, you can find the range of an expression built from it. To find the range of a sum, product, or difference, test the extreme combinations of endpoints.

Example: if 1 < x < 4 and 2 < y < 5, then x - y ranges from (smallest x − largest y) to (largest x − smallest y) = (1 - 5) to (4 - 2), so -4 < x - y < 2. Always pair the extremes carefully, especially with subtraction and division where signs can surprise you.

Comparing Values from Ranges (QC)

Quantitative Comparison loves inequalities because partial information often makes a comparison impossible to determine. If a range includes both positive and negative numbers, or straddles 1, expressions like x2, 1/x, or √x can behave very differently. Always test boundary and sign-changing values before concluding.
whatEtsTests:
• Flip-the-sign discipline: dividing or multiplying by a negative and remembering to reverse the inequality.
  • Absolute value as distance: translating |x - a| < k into a range and back.
  • The and/or distinction: |x| < k gives one interval; |x| > k gives two.
  • Range arithmetic in QC: combining two ranges, especially with subtraction or division, to compare quantities.
  • Cannot-be-determined traps: ranges that span zero or 1, where squaring, reciprocals, or roots flip the comparison.
  • No-solution / all-solution cases: absolute value set equal to or compared with a negative number.
  • Translating word problems: phrases like "at most," "no more than," "at least" into ≤ and ≥.
tips: When you divide or multiply by a negative, flip the sign immediately and circle it so you never forget.
Read |x - a| as 'distance from a' — it turns most absolute-value problems into a one-line range.
For |x| < k think AND (one between-range); for |x| > k think OR (two pieces). 'Less-thand, great-or.'
To find the range of x - y, compute (min x − max y) up to (max x − min y); never just subtract the matching ends.
On QC, test x = 0, a negative, a fraction between 0 and 1, and a large number — these four catch most flips.
If an absolute value equals or is less than a negative number, stop: there is no solution. If it's greater than a negative, all reals work.
traps: Forgetting to flip the inequality sign when multiplying or dividing by a negative.
Writing |x| > k as a single range instead of two separate pieces (x < -k OR x > k).
Assuming a range of x gives the same range for x² — squaring a range that includes negatives or fractions behaves differently.
In QC, concluding a comparison when the range spans both signs or crosses 1, where the answer is actually 'cannot be determined.'
Treating |x| = -k as having solutions; absolute value is never negative.

examples.q: Solve for x: 4 - 3x ≥ 13. Express the answer as a range.
examples.steps:
1. Subtract 4 from both sides: -3x ≥ 9.
  2. Divide both sides by -3. Because -3 is negative, flip the sign: x ≤ -3.
  3. So the solution is all x with x ≤ -3.
examples.shortcut: Move the variable to keep its coefficient positive: 4 - 13 ≥ 3x, so -9 ≥ 3x, giving -3 ≥ x. No flip needed because you never divided by a negative.

examples.q: Solve |2x - 5| < 9 and give the range of x.
examples.steps:
1. 'Less than' means a single between-range: -9 < 2x - 5 < 9.
  2. Add 5 to all three parts: -4 < 2x < 14.
  3. Divide all parts by 2: -2 < x < 7.
examples.shortcut: Distance view: |2x - 5| < 9 means 2x is within 9 of 5, so 2x is between -4 and 14, i.e. x between -2 and 7.

examples.q: If -2 < x < 3 and 1 < y < 4, what is the range of x - y?
examples.steps:
1. Smallest x - y uses smallest x and largest y: -2 - 4 = -6.
  2. Largest x - y uses largest x and smallest y: 3 - 1 = 2.
  3. So -6 < x - y < 2.
examples.shortcut: To subtract ranges, flip y's range to -4 < -y < -1, then add to x's range: (-2 + -4, 3 + -1) = (-6, 2).

examples.steps:
1. 'Greater than or equal' means two pieces (OR): x + 1 ≤ -4 OR x + 1 ≥ 4.
  2. Solve each: x ≤ -5 OR x ≥ 3.
  3. On a number line: filled dot at -5 shading left, and filled dot at 3 shading right.
examples.shortcut: Distance view: |x + 1| = |x - (-1)| is the distance from -1. We need distance ≥ 4, so x is at least 4 units from -1: x ≤ -5 or x ≥ 3.

questions.q: How many integer values of x satisfy |x| < 5?
questions.explain: |x| < 5 means -5 < x < 5. The integers are -4, -3, -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, which is 9 values.
Divide both sides by -3. Since -3 is negative, flip the sign: x ≤ -4.
'Greater than' splits into two pieces: x < -3 OR x > 3 (the points more than 3 units from 0).
Subtract 3 from all parts: -10 ≤ -2x < 2. Divide all parts by -2 and flip both signs (reversing the whole chain): 5 ≥ x > -1, i.e. -1 < x ≤ 5.

questions.q: If 5 + 3n ≤ 23, what is the greatest possible integer value of n?
questions.explain: Subtract 5: 3n ≤ 18. Divide by 3: n ≤ 6. The greatest integer is 6.
For any number strictly between 0 and 1, squaring makes it smaller (for example, 0.5² = 0.25). So x² < x throughout the range, and Quantity B is greater. The answer is B.

questions.q: The inequality |x - 4| ≤ 3 is equivalent to which of the following?
questions.explain: |x - 4| ≤ 3 means x is within 3 of 4: -3 ≤ x - 4 ≤ 3, so 1 ≤ x ≤ 7.

questions.q: If 3 < x < 8 and 1 < y < 4, which of the following could be the value of x − y?
questions.explain: The range of x − y is (min x − max y, max x − min y) = (3 − 4, 8 − 1) = (-1, 7), an open interval. Only 5 lies strictly inside it. The endpoints -1 and 7 are excluded (the inequalities are strict), and -5 and 9 are outside the interval.
Test the range. If x = 0, then x² = 0 < 1, so A is smaller. If x = 1.5, then x² = 2.25 > 1, so A is larger. The comparison changes depending on x, so it cannot be determined. The answer is D.

questions.info: It is given that a < b < 0.
questions.explain: Both a and b are negative with a more negative (smaller) than b. Taking reciprocals of negatives reverses order: for example a = -4, b = -2 gives 1/a = -0.25 and 1/b = -0.5, so 1/a > 1/b. This holds throughout: since |a| > |b|, the reciprocal of the larger magnitude is closer to 0, hence greater. Quantity A is greater. The answer is A.

questions.q: If |3 - x| < 5, which of the following values of x satisfy the inequality? Select all that apply.
questions.explain: |3 - x| < 5 means -5 < 3 - x < 5. Subtract 3: -8 < -x < 2. Multiply by -1 and flip: 8 > x > -2, i.e. -2 < x < 8. Values strictly inside: -1, 0, 5, 7. Excluded: -3 (too small) and 8 (endpoint, not <).
